skill.name: azure-keyvault-secrets-ts
skill.description: Use Azure Key Vault Secrets and Keys SDK examples to store, retrieve, rotate, back up, and recover application secrets in TypeScript.
skill.summary: Build TypeScript workflows for Azure Key Vault secrets, keys, rotation, and backup tasks.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged command execution, network, environment, sensitive file, weak crypto, and heuristic issues. Review found these are mostly Markdown SDK examples, not executable skill code, but the guide includes examples that print secret material and purge secrets or keys. No prompt injection, malicious network exfiltration, or confirmed blocked behavior was found.

medium_findings.title: Secret Material Printed in Examples
medium_findings.description: The guide shows secret and decrypted values being written to standard output. This is dangerous if copied into production code because logs can expose credentials or plaintext secrets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples explicitly output retrieved secret values and decrypted plaintext. The issue is a documentation safety risk, not evidence of malicious code.

medium_findings.title: Permanent Purge Operations Included
medium_findings.description: The guide includes purge calls for deleted secrets and keys. These are legitimate Azure Key Vault APIs, but users can permanently destroy recoverable material if they copy them without safeguards.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The purge operations are directly present in the examples. They are legitimate administrative actions, so the risk depends on user context.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The external command detections refer to fenced Markdown examples, including an npm install command and TypeScript SDK snippets. No repository script or runtime hook executes these examples automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only file is SKILL.md, and the flagged text appears inside documentation code fences. I found no executable wrapper or instruction that runs arbitrary shell commands.

low_findings.title: Network and Environment Access Are Azure SDK Configuration
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL and environment variable access build an Azure Key Vault endpoint from a vault name. This is expected for the documented SDK use and does not show exfiltration to an unrelated endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is the standard Azure Key Vault host pattern and the environment variable contains a vault name. No evidence found of sending secrets to a third-party domain.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Sensitive File Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak crypto flag points to metadata text, and the certificate or key file flag points to key object logging. The actual crypto examples use SHA-256, RSA-OAEP, and RS256.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines do not access certificate files or use a weak algorithm. The crypto examples shown are common Azure Key Vault SDK algorithms.

low_findings.title: Critical Heuristic Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The dangerous combination heuristic is not supported by semantic review. The file is a documentation guide for Azure Key Vault and contains no prompt injection, hidden instructions, or malicious data exfiltration pattern.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All detected patterns are visible documentation examples in one Markdown file. I found no evidence of prompt injection, obfuscation, or unauthorized network behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Logging Secret Values
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples demonstrate printing secret values and decrypted plaintext. Marketplace users should avoid logging secret material and should redact outputs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly visible in the documented examples. The concern is misuse after copying the snippets.

dangerous_patterns.title: Permanent Key Vault Purge Calls
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples include permanent deletion operations for secrets and keys. These actions should require explicit human approval and environment checks.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The purge calls are explicit and destructive. They are legitimate APIs but unsafe without operational controls.

content.user_title: Manage Azure Key Vault Secrets in TypeScript
content.value_statement: Teams need reliable patterns for handling application secrets without spreading credentials across code and logs. This skill provides Azure Key Vault TypeScript examples for secret, key, rotation, backup, and recovery workflows.
actual_capabilities: Shows installation steps for Azure Key Vault Secrets and Azure Identity packages.
Demonstrates DefaultAzureCredential setup with Azure Key Vault endpoint configuration.
Provides examples for setting, retrieving, listing, deleting, recovering, and purging secrets.
Includes key creation, listing, rotation policy, deletion, purge, backup, and restore examples.
Covers cryptographic operations such as encrypt, decrypt, sign, verify, wrap, and unwrap.
limitations: The skill is a reference guide, not an executable integration or deployed service.
Examples require Azure credentials, Key Vault access, and correct Azure SDK packages.
Some snippets use placeholder secret names and values that must be replaced safely.
The guide includes destructive purge examples that need explicit operational controls.

use_cases.title: Add Secret Retrieval to a Node Service
use_cases.description: Use the TypeScript examples to connect a backend service to Azure Key Vault with DefaultAzureCredential.

use_cases.title: Plan Key Rotation Workflows
use_cases.description: Adapt the rotation policy examples to define automatic key lifecycle behavior for production vaults.

use_cases.title: Review Secure SDK Usage
use_cases.description: Check application code against the guide before adding backup, restore, or cryptographic operations.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Secret Client
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to help me create a TypeScript Azure Key Vault SecretClient with DefaultAzureCredential. Explain the required environment variables.

prompt_templates.title: Add Secret Read and Write Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a TypeScript flow that stores and retrieves an application secret from Azure Key Vault without logging the secret value.

prompt_templates.title: Design Key Rotation Policy
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to draft a key rotation approach for Azure Key Vault, including expiration, rotation timing, and least privilege key operations.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Key Vault Implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my TypeScript Azure Key Vault code for unsafe logging, purge operations, credential handling, and missing error handling.

output_examples.input: Set up Azure Key Vault access in a TypeScript service.
output_examples.output: A concise implementation plan that lists package installation, credential setup, vault URL configuration, and SecretClient initialization steps.

output_examples.input: Review whether our secret retrieval flow is safe.
output: Confirms that secret values should not be printed or stored in logs.
Recommends redaction for diagnostics and scoped Azure permissions.
Identifies where recoverable delete is safer than purge.

output_examples.input: Plan a rotation policy for encryption keys.
output_examples.output: A rotation checklist covering key lifetime, automatic rotation timing, allowed operations, expiration dates, and recovery planning.

best_practices: Use managed identities or DefaultAzureCredential instead of hardcoded credentials.
Redact secret values and decrypted plaintext from logs, traces, and error messages.
Restrict purge, backup, restore, and rotation permissions to approved operators.
anti_patterns: Do not copy examples that print secret values into production code.
Do not grant broad Key Vault permissions to application identities.
Do not run purge operations without retention, approval, and recovery checks.

faq.question: Does this skill execute Azure commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It is a Markdown reference with TypeScript examples that users can adapt.

faq.question: Which Azure authentication method does it show?
faq.answer: It shows DefaultAzureCredential from the Azure Identity package.

faq.question: Can it manage both secrets and keys?
faq.answer: Yes. The guide includes examples for SecretClient, KeyClient, and CryptographyClient.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium risk warning because examples include secret logging and purge operations.

faq.question: Does it support browser applications?
faq.answer: No. The guide states these Azure SDK examples are for Node.js.

faq.question: What should users change before production use?
faq.answer: Users should remove secret logging, replace placeholders, restrict permissions, and add approval controls for destructive operations.
